title: Water and Wastewater Regulations Practice Test (Federal)
level: Entry to working level (ABC Class I-II)

questions.text: Under the federal secondary treatment standard, what is the minimum percent removal required for BOD5 and TSS on a 30-day average?
questions.explanation: The secondary treatment standard requires at least 85 percent removal of BOD5 and TSS, in addition to the 30 mg/L 30-day average concentration limits.

questions.text: What is the maximum residual disinfectant level (MRDL) for free chlorine in drinking water?
questions.explanation: The federal MRDL for free chlorine is 4.0 mg/L as Cl2, set to limit disinfectant exposure while maintaining disinfection.

questions.text: The Revised Total Coliform Rule changed how total coliform results are handled compared with the older Total Coliform Rule. Which statement correctly describes the current federal approach?
choices: Total coliforms have a maximum contaminant level of 5 percent positive, and E. coli has no standard
Both total coliforms and E. coli have maximum contaminant levels set at zero
Neither total coliforms nor E. coli is regulated once a system disinfects
Total coliforms no longer carry a maximum contaminant level and instead act as a treatment technique trigger, while E. coli has a maximum contaminant level with a goal of zero
questions.explanation: Under the Revised Total Coliform Rule, a total coliform detection is a trigger for assessment and follow-up rather than an automatic MCL violation. E. coli retains a maximum contaminant level with a maximum contaminant level goal of zero, because it is a more specific indicator of fecal contamination.
questions.citation: 40 CFR 141.63 + RTCR Subpart Y and the EPA RTCR page. The old total coliform MCL provisions in 141.63 apply only until March 31, 2016; from April 1, 2016 compliance is determined for the E. coli MCL. EPA RTCR: total coliform positives trigger Level 1 and Level 2 assessments (no MCL), and the E. coli MCLG is zero.

questions.text: What is the difference between an MCL and an MCLG?
choices: The MCLG is a non-enforceable health goal; the MCL is the enforceable limit set as close to the goal as feasible
They are two names for the same enforceable limit
The MCLG is enforceable; the MCL is only a guideline
The MCL applies to groundwater and the MCLG to surface water
questions.explanation: The maximum contaminant level goal is the health-based target and carries no enforcement. The maximum contaminant level is the legal limit, set as close to the goal as treatment technology and cost feasibly allow.

questions.text: Under the Safe Drinking Water Act, primacy means a state has...
choices: The right to set MCLs weaker than EPA's
Primary enforcement responsibility for drinking water regulations, with EPA oversight
Ownership of all public water systems
questions.explanation: Most states hold primacy: they adopt rules at least as strict as the federal ones and carry primary enforcement responsibility, while EPA keeps an oversight role.

questions.text: A water system must follow the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations if it is...
choices: A private well serving one home
Any system with a storage tank
Only a system serving a city
questions.explanation: The NPDWRs apply to public water systems. Individual rules then vary in their details by system type and source, but private domestic wells sit outside the SDWA entirely.

questions.text: What makes a water system a public water system under the SDWA?
choices: It is owned by a city or district
It serves at least 100 people year round
It serves at least 15 service connections or at least 25 people for at least 60 days a year
questions.explanation: The definition is numeric: 15 or more service connections, or 25 or more people, for at least 60 days per year. Ownership, public or private, does not matter.

questions.text: A factory runs its own well and serves water to the same 80 employees every workday. What kind of public water system is it?
questions.explanation: A non-transient non-community system serves at least 25 of the same people more than 6 months a year at a place they do not live, like a factory or school.

questions.text: Why is the MCLG for some contaminants set at zero?
choices: No level of exposure is considered free of risk for those contaminants
Treatment can always remove them completely
questions.explanation: For known and probable carcinogens the health goal is zero because no threshold is considered risk free. The enforceable MCL then lands as close to zero as is feasible.
questions.citation: EPA MCLG methodology; 40 CFR 141 subpart F and G

questions.text: What is the MCL for nitrate in drinking water, measured as nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Nitrate's MCL is 10 mg/L as N. Its companion nitrite is 1.0 mg/L as N. Nitrate is also one of the few acute, Tier 1 contaminants.

questions.text: What is the MCL for arsenic?
questions.explanation: Arsenic's MCL is 0.010 mg/L (10 micrograms per liter), tightened from 0.050 in the 2001 rule.

questions.text: Federal secondary drinking water standards, like the ones for iron and manganese, are...
choices: Non-enforceable guidelines for aesthetic qualities, though states may choose to enforce them
Enforceable federal limits with public notification
Limits that apply only to surface water systems
questions.explanation: The secondary standards address aesthetics such as iron, manganese, chloride, and pH. Federally they are guidance, not mandates, but many states enforce some of them.

questions.text: EPA sets a treatment technique instead of an MCL when...
choices: The contaminant is not a health risk
There is no economically or technically feasible way to measure the contaminant reliably at compliance levels
The contaminant only occurs in groundwater
questions.explanation: Lead and Cryptosporidium are the classic examples: instead of an MCL, the rule requires specific treatment or actions because routine compliance measurement is impractical.

questions.text: Operator certification programs for water systems are run by...
questions.explanation: States run operator certification under their own rules, which must meet EPA's baseline guidelines. That is why grades, eligibility, and renewal differ state to state.

questions.text: Who carries out routine compliance monitoring at a public water system?
choices: EPA inspectors on a federal schedule
The county health department in all cases
The system itself, reporting results to the state
questions.explanation: The system is responsible for collecting its compliance samples on schedule, using approved methods and labs, and reporting results to the state.

questions.text: Which federal law governs the quality of water delivered by public water systems?
choices: The Resource Conservation and Recovery Act
questions.explanation: The SDWA covers drinking water at public water systems. The Clean Water Act covers wastewater discharges and surface water quality. Knowing which law owns which side is a free exam point.

questions.text: The federal secondary treatment standard for TSS in a POTW's effluent, as a 30-day average, is...
questions.explanation: Secondary treatment means 30-day averages of 30 mg/L for both BOD5 and TSS, with 45 mg/L allowed as the 7-day average.

questions.text: Under the federal secondary treatment standards, the 7-day average limit for BOD5 is...
questions.explanation: The standards run in pairs: 30 mg/L as the 30-day average and 45 mg/L as the 7-day average, for both BOD5 and TSS.

questions.text: The federal secondary treatment standards require effluent pH to stay between...
questions.explanation: Secondary treatment effluent must stay between pH 6.0 and 9.0 unless the permit says otherwise. The 6.5 to 8.5 range belongs to the drinking water aesthetic standards.

questions.text: The 85 percent removal requirement in the secondary treatment standards is measured as...
choices: A 30-day average percent removal of BOD5 and TSS relative to influent concentrations
A daily minimum that can never be missed
An annual average across both parameters
questions.explanation: Secondary treatment requires removing at least 85 percent of incoming BOD5 and TSS as a 30-day average. Strong plants meet it easily; dilute influent is where it gets tricky.

questions.text: A trickling filter plant qualifying as equivalent to secondary treatment may be permitted BOD5 limits as high as...
choices: 30 mg/L as a 30-day average and 45 mg/L as a 7-day average
45 mg/L as a 30-day average and 65 mg/L as a 7-day average
50 mg/L as a 30-day average and 70 mg/L as a 7-day average
No relaxation is allowed for any technology
questions.explanation: Facilities using trickling filters or waste stabilization ponds that meet the conditions can be classed as equivalent to secondary, with 45 mg/L 30-day and 65 mg/L 7-day BOD5 limits and a lower removal floor.

questions.text: Where do the federal secondary treatment standards for POTWs live?
questions.explanation: Secondary treatment standards sit in 40 CFR Part 133. Knowing the neighborhood (141 drinking water, 122 NPDES, 403 pretreatment) earns easy points.

questions.text: Untreated sewage escaping a separate sanitary sewer and reaching a creek is...
choices: Legal if it happens during heavy rain
Allowed if reported within a year
Only a problem if fish are killed
A prohibited discharge under the Clean Water Act
questions.explanation: SSOs that reach surface waters violate the Clean Water Act because no permit authorizes them. Weather does not excuse the discharge, and the system must still report and respond to it.

questions.text: What is the difference between a CSO and an SSO?
choices: A CSO is a permitted wet-weather discharge from a combined sewer; an SSO is an unpermitted overflow from a separate sanitary sewer
They are two names for the same event
An SSO is always smaller than a CSO
A CSO happens only at the treatment plant
questions.explanation: Combined sewer overflows discharge from designed outfalls under NPDES permits and the federal CSO policy. Sanitary sewer overflows have no permit and are violations.

questions.text: The main purpose of an industrial pretreatment program is to...
choices: Keep industrial discharges from passing through or interfering with the POTW and protect workers and the plant
Eliminate the need for an NPDES permit
questions.explanation: Pretreatment controls what industries put into the sewer so it cannot pass through the plant untreated, upset the process, contaminate biosolids, or endanger collection workers.

questions.text: A metal finisher wants to discharge rinse water to the sanitary sewer. What regulates that discharge?
choices: Nothing, once it enters the sewer it is the city's problem
An NPDES permit issued to the finisher for the sewer discharge
The pretreatment program: federal categorical standards plus the POTW's local limits
questions.explanation: Industries discharging to a POTW fall under pretreatment: EPA categorical standards for their industry plus local limits the POTW sets to protect its plant and permit.

questions.text: The permit that authorizes a treatment plant to discharge treated effluent to a river is...
questions.explanation: Point-source discharges to waters of the United States require a National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit, issued by the state where authorized, or EPA.

questions.text: Standard NPDES permit conditions require reporting any noncompliance that may endanger health or the environment within...
choices: 24 hours of becoming aware of it, orally, with a written report to follow
One year, in the annual report
questions.explanation: Permits carry standard conditions: noncompliance that may endanger health or the environment gets an oral report within 24 hours and a written follow-up, typically within 5 days.

questions.text: A routine distribution sample comes back total coliform-positive one time. Under the RTCR, what does that single result trigger on its own?
choices: An automatic MCL violation requiring Tier 1 notice
Not an MCL violation by itself; it triggers repeat sampling
Ignored unless turbidity is also high
A reason to shut the system down
questions.explanation: Total coliform positives start the response ladder: repeat samples, and if results keep coming back positive, an assessment. The MCL itself now belongs to E. coli only.

questions.text: A routine sample comes back E. coli positive. The repeat samples are total coliform positive but E. coli negative. What is the compliance result?
choices: No violation, since the repeats were E. coli negative
questions.explanation: The E. coli MCL is violated by defined combinations, including an E. coli-positive routine followed by a total coliform-positive repeat. The repeats going E. coli negative does not clear it.

questions.text: After a total coliform-positive routine sample, a system must collect how many repeat samples?
choices: 5, by the end of the month
At least 3, within 24 hours of being notified
questions.explanation: Every total coliform positive triggers at least three repeat samples collected within 24 hours of learning the result.

questions.text: Where are repeat samples collected after a coliform positive?
choices: At the original tap and at the repeat monitoring sites named in the system's sample siting plan
At the farthest point in the system
Anywhere the operator chooses that day
questions.explanation: Under the current Revised Total Coliform Rule (RTCR), repeats go back to the original tap plus the repeat monitoring locations the system named in its own sample siting plan, approved by the primacy agency. There is no separate number to memorize; the plan controls.

questions.text: What triggers a Level 1 assessment under the RTCR?
choices: Exceeding the total coliform treatment technique trigger, such as multiple TC positives in a month
questions.explanation: Level 1 assessments respond to the treatment technique trigger: for small systems, two or more TC positives in a month; for large ones, more than 5.0 percent positive.

questions.text: What is the difference between a Level 1 and a Level 2 assessment?
choices: Level 2 is just a re-run of Level 1 paperwork
Level 1 requires shutting down a well
Level 2 is more in-depth and must be done by the state or a state-approved party, for E. coli violations or repeat triggers
There is no difference in practice
questions.explanation: Level 1 is a self-assessment of obvious sanitary defects. Level 2 digs deeper, follows E. coli violations or repeated triggers, and needs state-approved expertise.

questions.text: The written document that says where a system collects its routine and repeat coliform samples is called...
questions.explanation: Every system must keep a written sample siting plan covering routine and repeat locations, subject to state review.

questions.text: How is the number of routine coliform samples a system must collect each month determined?
choices: By the population the system serves
By the age of the distribution system
By how many operators are certified
questions.explanation: Routine coliform monitoring frequency comes from a population-based table, starting at one sample per month for systems serving 1,000 or fewer.

questions.text: For a system collecting at least 40 routine samples per month, the total coliform treatment technique trigger is exceeded when...
choices: Any sample is total coliform positive
More than 5.0 percent of monthly samples are total coliform positive
questions.explanation: Large systems trip the trigger above 5.0 percent TC-positive in a month; systems under 40 samples trip it at two or more positives.

questions.text: An E. coli MCL violation requires what kind of public notification?
questions.explanation: E. coli MCL violations are Tier 1: the public must be notified within 24 hours through broadcast media, hand delivery, or posting as the state directs.

questions.text: A seasonal campground system reopens each spring. Before serving water, the RTCR requires it to...
choices: Collect 10 routine samples in the first week
Complete state-approved start-up procedures and certify that it did
questions.explanation: Seasonal systems depressurize and sit idle, so the rule requires documented start-up procedures before serving water each season.

questions.text: For conventional and direct filtration plants, combined filter effluent turbidity must be...
choices: 0.3 NTU or less in at least 95 percent of monthly measurements
1.0 NTU or less in every measurement with no other requirement
5 NTU or less at all times
questions.explanation: Conventional and direct filtration must keep combined filter effluent at or below 0.3 NTU in 95 percent of the measurements each month.

questions.text: The combined filter effluent turbidity level that conventional plants may never exceed is...
questions.explanation: Alongside the 0.3 NTU 95 percent standard, combined filter effluent must never exceed 1 NTU at conventional and direct filtration plants.

questions.text: The Surface Water Treatment Rule requires total removal or inactivation of Giardia and viruses of at least...
choices: 3-log (99.9 percent) for Giardia and 4-log (99.99 percent) for viruses
questions.explanation: Treatment must achieve 99.9 percent (3-log) Giardia and 99.99 percent (4-log) virus removal or inactivation, split between filtration credit and disinfection CT.

questions.text: Under LT2, the Long Term 2 Enhanced Surface Water Treatment Rule, how much Cryptosporidium removal credit do conventional filtration plants receive?
choices: No credit regardless of how well the filters perform
6-log automatically with no source water monitoring required
1-log baseline adjusted only when turbidity runs low
3-log, with additional requirements possible based on source water monitoring
questions.explanation: Under LT2 conventional filtration in compliance with existing turbidity requirements is credited 3-log Cryptosporidium removal. The 40 CFR 141.711 bin table shows it: Bin 4 total treatment is 5.5-log and its additional requirement is 2.5-log. LT2 then bins systems by source water Cryptosporidium monitoring results and can require additional treatment above that credit.

questions.text: In disinfection compliance, CT stands for...
choices: Disinfectant residual concentration multiplied by contact time
questions.explanation: CT is concentration times time: the residual leaving the contact zone in mg/L times the minutes of contact, compared against required CT tables for the target organism and conditions.

questions.text: Which systems must comply with the Surface Water Treatment Rules?
choices: Systems using surface water or groundwater under the direct influence of surface water
questions.explanation: The SWTR family applies to any public water system using surface water or groundwater under the direct influence of surface water (GWUDI).

questions.text: How often must conventional plants measure combined filter effluent turbidity?
choices: At least every 4 hours, or continuously
questions.explanation: Combined filter effluent turbidity must be measured at least every four hours during operation; continuous monitoring may substitute with state approval.

questions.text: A surface water system that wants to avoid filtration entirely must...
choices: Maintain continuous disinfection alone, without meeting turbidity or coliform criteria
Serve a population under 500, regardless of source water quality
Meet the turbidity limit alone, with no coliform or watershed criteria
Meet strict source water and site criteria, and keep disinfecting
questions.explanation: Unfiltered systems exist only where source water meets avoidance criteria continuously, with watershed control and disinfection still required. Miss the criteria and filtration becomes mandatory.

questions.text: Individual filter turbidity monitoring at conventional plants requires...
choices: Continuous monitoring of each filter, recorded at least every 15 minutes
A weekly grab sample per filter
questions.explanation: Each filter gets its own continuous turbidimeter with results recorded at least every 15 minutes, because one bad filter can hide inside a clean combined average.

questions.text: The MCL for total trihalomethanes (TTHM) is...
questions.explanation: TTHM's MCL is 0.080 mg/L (80 micrograms per liter), evaluated as a locational running annual average under Stage 2.

questions.text: The MCL for the five regulated haloacetic acids (HAA5) is...
questions.explanation: HAA5's MCL is 0.060 mg/L (60 micrograms per liter), the partner number to TTHM's 80.

questions.text: Under Stage 2, TTHM and HAA5 compliance is calculated as...
choices: A system-wide average across all sites
The worst single result of the year
A locational running annual average at each monitoring site
questions.explanation: Each monitoring location must meet the MCL as its own running annual average (LRAA). Averaging across the system, the Stage 1 approach, no longer applies.

questions.text: Under the Stage 2 DBPR, what is the MRDL ceiling for free chlorine residual in the distribution system?
choices: 4.0 mg/L as Cl2, as an annual average
questions.explanation: Chlorine's MRDL is 4.0 mg/L measured as a running annual average of distribution samples. It caps how much disinfectant residual customers can be exposed to long term.

questions.text: Disinfection byproducts like TTHMs form when...
choices: Pipes corrode in low pH water
Chlorine reacts with natural organic matter in the water
questions.explanation: Free chlorine reacting with natural organic matter creates trihalomethanes and haloacetic acids. That is why precursor removal and chloramine strategies exist.
questions.citation: EPA Stage 1/Stage 2 DBPR background; 40 CFR 141 subpart V

questions.text: Stage 2 DBP monitoring locations were chosen based on...
choices: The oldest pipes in the system
An evaluation finding the highest-DBP sites in the distribution system
questions.explanation: Systems ran an Initial Distribution System Evaluation to locate where DBPs run highest, and those sites became the Stage 2 compliance monitoring locations.

questions.text: Lead and copper are regulated with a treatment technique instead of an MCL because...
choices: They enter water from plumbing and service lines after treatment, past any plant-side limit
Levels vary too much sample to sample for one plant-side limit to work
Corrosion control only fixes taste and color, not the health risk
questions.explanation: Lead and copper leach from service lines, solder, and fixtures between the plant and the tap. The rule therefore requires corrosion control and tap monitoring rather than an MCL at the entry point.

questions.text: The action level for copper at the tap is...
questions.explanation: Copper's action level is 1.3 mg/L, evaluated at the 90th percentile of tap samples.

questions.text: Whether a system exceeds a lead or copper action level is decided by...
choices: The 90th percentile of its tap sample results
questions.explanation: Rank the tap results; if the 90th percentile result tops the action level, the system has an exceedance and the required actions kick in.

questions.text: Exceeding a lead or copper action level is...
choices: An MCL violation requiring Tier 1 notice within 24 hours
Not an MCL violation, but a treatment-technique trigger
questions.explanation: An action level exceedance is a trigger, not an MCL violation. It obligates the system to act: corrosion control treatment steps, monitoring, and for lead, public education. One more wrinkle: under the Lead and Copper Rule Revisions (LCRR), a lead action level exceedance also triggers a Tier 1 public notice within 24 hours (141.202(a)(10)), even though it is still not an MCL violation.

questions.text: Lead and copper compliance samples are collected...
choices: First-draw samples from taps at higher-risk sites
At the treatment plant entry point
questions.explanation: Samples come from customer taps at sites most likely to have lead exposure, such as homes with lead service lines or lead solder, collected first-draw so the water has been sitting in the plumbing. Under the Lead and Copper Rule Improvements (LCRI), lead service line sites also add a paired fifth-liter sample that targets the service line itself, on top of the standard first-liter sample.

questions.text: Common corrosion control treatments under the Lead and Copper Rule include...
choices: Increasing chlorine residual to a target of 2 mg/L or higher
Adjusting pH and alkalinity, or feeding a phosphate corrosion inhibitor
Softening with lime alone, without checking the finished pH
Adding fluoride at the system's target dose for dental health
questions.explanation: Systems control corrosivity by raising pH or alkalinity, or feeding orthophosphate to form a protective scale inside pipes.

questions.text: A customer asks why the utility had them run the tap sample themselves. The accurate answer is...
choices: Resident sampling is illegal but cheaper
Residents may collect their own first-draw samples when the utility gives instructions
Only the state may collect tap samples
questions.explanation: Residents may collect their own first-liter (first-draw) samples following the system's instructions. That captures water stagnant at least six hours, which best represents lead exposure, which is exactly why the utility asked them to do it that way.

questions.text: After a lead action level exceedance, a system must deliver...
choices: A Tier 3 notice within one year
Free bottled water to every customer
A rate refund credited to the next billing cycle
Public education about lead health effects and exposure reduction
questions.explanation: A lead action level exceedance obligates the system to run the rule's public education program, telling customers about health effects, sources, and how to limit exposure.

questions.text: The most common source of lead in drinking water is...
choices: Lead plumbing materials between the main and the tap
The source water aquifer, before any treatment happens
Chemicals added during treatment at the plant
Flushing hydrants to clear the mains
questions.explanation: Lead enters between the main and the glass: lead service lines, pre-1986 solder, and leaded brass. Corrosive water pulls it into solution, which is why corrosion control works.
questions.citation: 40 CFR 141 subpart I; EPA lead guidance

questions.text: Under the Clean Water Act, discharging a pollutant from a point source to waters of the United States without a permit is...
questions.explanation: The CWA starts from a prohibition: no point-source discharge to waters of the United States except in compliance with a permit. Everything else in the act hangs off that.

questions.text: The self-monitoring results a permitted facility submits to its regulator are reported on a...
questions.explanation: NPDES permittees self-monitor and report results on Discharge Monitoring Reports at the frequency the permit sets, typically monthly.

questions.text: The maximum term of an NPDES permit before it must be reissued is...
questions.explanation: NPDES permits run at most five years. Facilities apply for renewal before expiration, and a timely application keeps the old permit in force while the new one is processed.

questions.text: Effluent limits in an NPDES permit are based on...
choices: Technology-based standards, tightened further where needed to protect the receiving water's quality standards
Whatever the plant can comfortably meet
questions.explanation: Permits start from technology-based limits (like secondary treatment for POTWs) and add water-quality-based limits where the receiving water's designated uses demand more.

questions.text: In most states, who issues NPDES permits?
choices: The state environmental agency, under EPA-authorized delegation
questions.explanation: EPA has authorized most states to run the NPDES program, so the state environmental agency issues and enforces permits, with EPA oversight.

questions.text: A bypass that causes effluent limitations to be exceeded is allowed under an NPDES permit only when...
choices: High wet-weather flows exceeded the plant's design capacity so operators routed the excess around one unit
It was done for essential maintenance to keep the plant operating efficiently
It was unavoidable to prevent loss of life, injury, or severe property damage, no feasible alternative existed, and the required notice was given
The utility reported the bypass to the state within 24 hours after it happened
questions.explanation: A bypass that causes an exceedance is prohibited unless it was unavoidable to prevent loss of life, injury, or severe property damage, there was no feasible alternative, and the utility gave the required notice. A bypass that does not cause an exceedance has a separate, broader allowance: it is allowed for essential maintenance to keep the plant running efficiently.

questions.text: Sample collection and analysis for NPDES compliance must follow...
choices: The drinking water methods at Part 141
Methods the permittee invents and documents
The methods approved at 40 CFR Part 136
questions.explanation: NPDES monitoring must use the analytical methods approved in 40 CFR Part 136 unless the permit specifies otherwise.

questions.text: Water quality standards for a receiving stream are built from...
choices: The best treatment technology each discharger can afford
One flat federal numeric limit applied to every stream nationwide
A public vote among residents near the water body
questions.explanation: States designate uses for each water body and adopt criteria to protect them. Those standards then drive water-quality-based permit limits upstream.

questions.text: Knowingly submitting false information on a Discharge Monitoring Report is...
questions.explanation: Discharge Monitoring Reports are certified under penalty of law when signed (40 CFR 122.22(d)). Knowingly falsifying one is a criminal matter under CWA 309(c) that has put real operators and managers in legal trouble. There is no grace period for a later correction and no audit-only exception. Record what actually happened, always.

questions.text: Municipal storm sewer systems (MS4s) and many industrial sites are regulated under...
questions.explanation: The CWA brings stormwater under NPDES too: municipal separate storm sewer systems, industrial facilities, and construction sites all carry stormwater permits.

questions.text: A community water system crosses AWIA's 3,300-person size threshold. From that point, federal law requires the system to...
choices: Build a second treatment plant as a backup
Hire armed security guards at every facility
File a daily security report with EPA
Complete a risk assessment and emergency response plan
questions.explanation: AWIA section 2013 requires CWSs over 3,300 served to assess risks and resilience and keep an emergency response plan that reflects the assessment.

questions.text: Under AWIA, the emergency response plan must be updated...
choices: Within six months of each RRA certification
Only after an EPA site inspection
Every month, regardless of the assessment
Never, since it is a one-time filing
questions.explanation: Each time the risk and resilience assessment is recertified, the emergency response plan must be reviewed, updated as needed, and certified within six months.

questions.text: How often must AWIA risk and resilience assessments be reviewed and recertified?
questions.explanation: Risk and resilience assessments recertify on a five-year cycle, keeping the analysis current as threats and the system change.

questions.text: An operator discovers signs that someone tampered with a well house overnight. After securing the scene, the right move is...
choices: Wait to see if water quality changes
Post about it on social media
Follow the emergency response plan's notification chain, including the state drinking water program and local response partners
Handle it quietly to avoid alarm
questions.explanation: Suspected tampering is exactly what the ERP exists for: isolate if needed, notify the state program and response partners per the plan, and document the event.

questions.text: A contractor cross-connects a chemical line and product backflows into the distribution system. Beyond stopping it, the utility must...
choices: Log it for the annual report only
Bill the contractor and close the file
Treat it as an emergency: isolate the area, notify the state, and warn affected customers
questions.explanation: Backflow of a chemical into the mains is an acute event: isolate, flush, sample, notify the state, and get appropriate notice to affected customers fast.
questions.citation: State backflow/emergency response requirements; 40 CFR 141 subpart Q

questions.text: The state's periodic onsite review of a water system's sources, treatment, storage, and operations is called...
questions.explanation: States conduct sanitary surveys on a multi-year cycle, walking the whole system from source to storage and flagging deficiencies that must be corrected.

questions.text: An unlocked hatch on a finished water storage tank is best described as...
choices: A sanitary defect and security risk that needs correction and inspection
Acceptable if the tank is tall
A problem only after a storm
questions.explanation: Hatches, vents, and overflows on finished water storage must be secured and screened. An unlocked hatch is a classic sanitary survey deficiency and a security gap.
questions.citation: EPA sanitary survey guidance; state design standards

questions.text: The operator's everyday contribution to system security is...
choices: Carrying out background checks on coworkers
Controlling access, noticing and reporting anything unusual, and keeping records of it
questions.explanation: Most security value comes from operators: locked facilities, challenge strangers, note vehicles and events, and report up the chain promptly.
questions.citation: AWIA ERP practices; EPA security guidance for small systems

questions.text: Community water systems must deliver a Consumer Confidence Report to customers...
questions.explanation: Every community water system publishes a CCR each year by July 1, covering the previous calendar year's water quality.

questions.text: A customer opens the water quality report their utility mails out every year. What is that Consumer Confidence Report built to show them?
choices: What contaminants were detected and how the results compare to the standards
questions.explanation: The CCR covers the system's sources, detected contaminants against their MCLs, any violations, and required educational language.

questions.text: How long must a water system keep its bacteriological analysis records?
questions.explanation: Records of bacteriological analyses are kept at least 5 years; chemical analyses at least 10.

questions.text: How long must records of chemical analyses be retained?
questions.explanation: Chemical analysis records are kept at least 10 years, twice the bacteriological retention.

questions.text: Records of lead and copper tap sampling and related actions must be kept for...
questions.explanation: Lead and Copper Rule records, sampling, treatment decisions, and related documentation, are retained for no fewer than 12 years.

questions.text: Routine compliance monitoring results are reported to the state within...
choices: 10 days after the end of the required monitoring period, unless a rule says otherwise
questions.explanation: The default is reporting results within the first 10 days following the monitoring period (or 10 days after receiving results), with specific rules setting their own schedules.

questions.text: A system that fails to comply with an applicable drinking water requirement must report that failure to the state within...
questions.explanation: Any failure to comply with a primary drinking water regulation must be reported to the state within 48 hours.

questions.text: Copies of public notices a system issued must be kept for...
questions.explanation: Systems keep copies of each public notification, and certifications to the state, for at least 3 years after issuance.

questions.text: On the wastewater side, NPDES monitoring records and DMR backup data must be retained for...
questions.explanation: NPDES permittees keep monitoring records, calibration and maintenance data, and required reports for at least 3 years from the date of the sample, measurement, report, or application (40 CFR 122.41(j)(2)). The Director can extend that period at any time, and many state programs or specific permits set a longer floor.

questions.text: Copies of written reports, summaries, or communications about a sanitary survey must be kept for...
questions.explanation: Copies of written reports, summaries, or communications about a sanitary survey are retained at least 10 years after the survey (40 CFR 141.33(c)). That is different from the 3-year retention for records of corrective actions taken to fix a violation (141.33(b)), so check which record type a question is asking about.

questions.text: The best reason to keep complete, dated operator logs and bench sheets is...
choices: They are only needed if an inspector asks for them
They satisfy the paperwork requirement, not the compliance requirement
They are the legal record that the work got done
They replace the need for permits entirely
questions.explanation: Logs and bench sheets are legal records that prove both that the work happened and that the results reported were real, not just that paperwork got filled out. Complete, contemporaneous, and honest entries are what stands between an operator and an unprovable claim when something is challenged later.

questions.text: A Tier 1 public notice must reach the public within...
questions.explanation: Tier 1 notices, for violations posing acute health risk, must go out within 24 hours via broadcast media, hand delivery, posting, or as the state directs.

questions.text: Which of these triggers a Tier 1 notice?
questions.explanation: Tier 1 covers acute risks: E. coli MCL violations, nitrate and nitrite MCL violations, and others the state designates.

questions.text: Most MCL violations that are not acute require a Tier 2 notice within...
questions.explanation: Non-acute MCL and treatment technique violations are Tier 2: notice within 30 days, with repeats every 3 months while the violation continues.

questions.text: Monitoring and testing procedure violations generally require which notice?
questions.explanation: Monitoring and testing violations are Tier 3: notify within 12 months, and the CCR can carry the notice if it meets the content rules.

questions.text: Acceptable delivery for a Tier 1 notice includes...
choices: A note in the next quarterly bill
An update buried on the website
Broadcast media, hand delivery, and conspicuous posting, as the situation and state require
questions.explanation: Tier 1 uses the fastest practical channels: radio and TV, hand delivery, posting in conspicuous locations, plus whatever else the state directs.

questions.text: An apartment complex gets its water through one master meter. Who must the public notice reach?
choices: All persons served, not just the billed account
Only the property owner who pays the bill
Only the household members of the account holder
questions.explanation: Public notification must reach all persons served. For master-metered properties that means posting or distribution that actually reaches tenants, not just the bill payer.

questions.text: A boil water advisory is...
choices: A voluntary suggestion with no rules attached
A common form of Tier 1 notice telling customers to boil water before use during an acute risk
A replacement for fixing the problem
questions.explanation: When an acute microbial risk exists, the Tier 1 notice often takes the form of a boil water advisory, issued fast and lifted only after confirmation sampling.
questions.citation: 40 CFR 141 subpart Q; state advisory protocols

questions.text: While a Tier 2 violation remains unresolved, the notice must be repeated...
questions.explanation: An unresolved Tier 2 violation requires repeat notice every 3 months, keeping customers informed for as long as the problem persists.

questions.text: What is the difference between the CCR and public notification?
choices: They are the same report; only the file name differs
The CCR goes to the state; notification goes to EPA
The CCR ended in 2019; notification took over
The CCR is the yearly report; notification is event-driven
questions.explanation: The CCR runs every year regardless of problems. Public notification fires when a violation or situation requires telling people, on the clock its tier sets.

questions.text: A combined sewer carries both sanitary sewage and stormwater in one pipe. During heavy rain, why does that system discharge a combined sewer overflow (CSO)?
choices: The utility chooses to dump excess flow to avoid paying treatment costs
State law requires a discharge whenever it rains more than an inch
The stormwater portion is illegal to treat, so it must bypass the plant
The pipe network and treatment plant cannot handle the combined wet-weather volume, so the excess discharges through a designed overflow point
questions.explanation: Combined systems carry sanitary sewage and stormwater together. When wet weather pushes the combined volume past what the pipes and plant can carry, the excess discharges through a permitted outfall built for that purpose, instead of backing up into streets and basements.
questions.citation: CWA Section 402(q); EPA CSO Control Policy (59 FR 18688, 1994)

questions.text: A collection system operator finds wastewater surcharging out of a manhole onto a street on a clear, dry day with no rain in the forecast. How must this event be classified and handled?
choices: As a CSO, since any overflow from a sewer is a combined sewer overflow
As a routine maintenance event that does not need to be reported
As a permitted bypass, since the plant was likely at capacity
As a sanitary sewer overflow: an unpermitted discharge that must be reported and addressed regardless of weather
questions.explanation: Only combined systems have permitted wet-weather overflow points, and this is a dry-weather event, which rules that out. A separate sanitary sewer has no authorized overflow condition, so any surcharge from it is a sanitary sewer overflow: an unpermitted discharge under the Clean Water Act that the system must report and respond to.
